title: 2025/08/14 - FD
displayTitle: 2025-08-14 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - Briefing on meetings with non-governmental organisations and individuals
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: Briefing on meetings with non-governmental organisations and individuals
description: 14 August 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Briefing on meetings with non-governmental organisations and individuals Asia New Zealand Foundation Suzannah Jessep, Chief Executive Dr Julia Macdonald, Director Research and Engagement
descriptionSummary: Briefing on meetings with non-governmental organisations and individuals. Asia New Zealand Foundation. Suzannah Jessep, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Excellent. All right, welcome along to the Foreign Affairs Defense and Trade Committee. Thank you again for our annual catch up delve into the perceptions of Asia Report. Um, great to have you along with us today. Thank you. We have half an hour sit down for this morning's discussion. Um, welcome to hand over to you for some introductory comments, and then I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions from members around the room. Thank You. Great. Fabulous.

Thank you, chair. Thank you. Honorable members. Uh, really lovely to have this opportunity to present Brighton early. Yes, thanks. Uh, I'm Suzanne Jessup, chief Executive of the Foundation, joined by my colleague, uh, Dr. Julie McDonald, who runs our research and track two, uh, engagement program.

Uh, we are here to share insights on our longitudinal research, the annual perceptions of Asia and Asian peoples. But if it's okay, I'd like to spend, um, the first seven minutes, uh, describing how, um, the work we do, uh, helps broader, uh, strategic priorities for New Zealand and helps build and support New Zealand's all important Asia relations.

Um, Julia will then spend about the same amount of time, and we should have 15 minutes for questions, if that's okay. Great. Great. Um, so who we are a non-profit, non-partisan, uh, expert organization focused on helping New Zealanders understand and connect to Asia. Um, we've been doing this work for 30 years, and we are currently a staff of 24.

Um, we work across government, uh, business and civil society, um, both here and across Asia. Um, obviously over the 30 years, our programs have evolved a lot. Um, but we currently run eight programs where we are targeting, um, uh, young New Zealanders and building, um, a pipeline of Asia capable Kiwis, uh, both here and across Asia.

Um, we also run the Asia Media Center, which is New Zealand's largest dedicated Asia news site. Um, our networks, uh, we have 550 young leaders and 280 entrepreneurs across New Zealand, Southeast Asia, and India. Uh, as you will all know, Asia is home to 60% of the world's population, and by 2032 thirds of, uh, global consumers will be Asian.

Um, the region has a third of the world's top universities, and those universities are going, um, you know, those countries that host those universities are currently going from net exporter of students that have ambitions to be importers. Um, Asia contains nearly 60% of the world's global Gen Z population.

Uh, this younger generation who are currently in their teens and twenties is obviously tech savvy shaped by digital media and whose careers will be profoundly influenced by ai. Um, at the same time, um, I'm sure this committee knows, uh, the region is facing tensions, military build ups, um, unprecedented military expenditure, tariffs and protectionism, um, major power competition, shifting alliances and so on.

And part of our job is to not only help shine a light on these changes and why they matter to the broader public, but also to understand how New Zealanders are perceiving those changes so that it helps guide our work. But it also helps decision makers such as yourself, so you understand your constituents and how the New Zealand public more broadly is seeing these issues.

So we support New Zealand's Asia engagement in three key ways. The first is that we are very focused on building an Asia capable workforce. We offer internships across Asia, their business internships, media internships, and other internships that are giving young New Zealanders real time in-country experience to hone their skills in this all important region.

We offer targeted career development and networking. Um, we have, uh, a big network of people like, um, Mor will be hosting this afternoon, the head of KPMG and Van and um, Vietnam, uh, who we can then introduce to this pipeline and really make sure they have valuable connections across the region.

Secondly, we are supporting businesses, sector leaders and creative industries, sports sector and others to build their Asia knowledge and their Asia connections because we, um, can be right across sectors. Our job is to really know the Asia region, and we are taking, uh, decision makers and sector leaders, pulling them together, creative sectors, sports sectors, showing them what the future trends are across Asia, which countries are interesting for them, why, who they should speak to and so on.

Um, we obviously can't do that, um, for all New Zealanders, so we are never trying to, with 24 people trying to reach four, 5 million New Zealanders, but we try and use that as a demonstration effect and then, um, let them lead across their sectors. And third, and finally, we're helping to strengthen New Zealand's, um, and deepen bilateral relationships.

So we run delegations, publish research, support, media outreach, host leadership programs, um, and our track two diplomacy program, which Julia leads, uh, makes sure that we have a network of candid, free and frank dialogues right across the region, um, where we are not misconstrued as government.

So we are getting free and frank assessments. So it might be that country ex says to us, you know, in New Zealand you're saying this, but actually this is what we see, and it means that we can put those messages back into the New Zealand system, uh, and they can then adjust, um, and, and bear that in mind with their policy with their country.

It also means we might get, um, told what countries are thinking about and doing in a way that's perhaps a little bit ahead of government, but it gives us early warning signs of, um, you know, from even yesterday we're talking career and tech and nuclear weapons. That just depends what the topic is. Um, so to give you a quick example, um, for example, in the last, uh, five to six months, if you take a country like Vietnam, um, we have published a report in support of leader level engagement.

So really, um, would you actually grab, um, this type of report? We were, we are making sure it's ready for high level engagement backed with stats, things that New Zealanders need to know on the relationship that gives anyone who's looking at Vietnam, um, and say, celebrating the 50th anniversary, um, or proper well-researched contemporary, um, information on say Vietnam.

Uh, we've hosted Vietnam's Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs, uh, provided a platform for a public engagement, um, reported a podcast. So we're bringing the New Zealand public along with these relationships. Why is New Zealand government investing in them? We are helping tell that story to the New Zealand public. Um, we've host, we've run media coverage and track two dialogues with the Diplomatic Academy of Vietnam who are vietnams leading thinkers and share those, those insights.

Uh, we have taken a creative delegation to Vietnam, who, which has then led to residencies. So we sort of try and create this, um, snowball effect. Last month we brought together 130 entrepreneurs in Vietnam who are the future of our trade relationship. Um, with this dynamic country, uh, demand for our programs is growing.

Um, when you look at internships alone in the last year, demand's grown by just over 800%. This tells us that New Zealanders, but importantly, young New Zealanders are seeing Asia skills and capabilities as valuable to their future. Um, so that on the whole, but they, they want these opportunities to be targeted and useful or sort of market ready skills.

That on the whole is a good trend, although we are seeing the erosion of Asia expertise across New Zealand universities. Um, and you know, that's sort of out of step with the macro trends, um, but it also shows us that we certainly at the foundation are able to provide a service that keeps young talent in New Zealand and that we can provide, um, real time opportunities that hone their skills in a way that's directly valuable to New Zealand public and private sector.

Uh, casting out more broadly, a research shows that over 80% of the New Zealand public now sees Asia building Asia relationships, political economic security as important to our future, um, up from 67% just a few years ago. So the also at the wider New Zealand, uh, picture the trend is quite clear.

So we know New Zealand for New Zealand Asia relationships are front and center. Um, we are small, but we've got strong nu numbers. Um, and just to finish up, to give you a sense of this, uh, for POA alone, uh, during our launch between the 20th and 30th of June, 2025, the report got 1.39 million impressions and just over 14,000 users opening and engaging with our live data tool.

Um, the report generated hundreds of online engagements with New Zealanders sharing their stories about how they have connected with the region, and that in itself stimulating a public discussion on these relationships. The report's been presented at Lowy, Melbourne University, national University of Singapore, and has been picked up by New Zealand Media and across, um, New Zealand embassies, uh, based here.

Um, I'll finish there, that seven minutes. We really appreciate this opportunity to present to you. I'll pass to Julia to now go into the specifics of the report. Thanks, SU Moreno Kozo. Um, I see that you all have a copy of the survey in front of you. Um, it's in its 28th year now and it provides the foundation and New Zealand really with a key way of tracking New Zealanders views of the region of different countries and of connections, um, to Asia as a whole.

So each year, two thirds of the questions remain the same. That's to preserve the longitudinal value of the survey. And one third we can change up each year to be more agile, flexible, respond to events and issues in the region. This year's survey took place between November and December, 2024 because a lot changed between December and the planned release date of June.

We decided to update some of the statistics and we released a mini poll in March, 2025 to retest some of the key questions. And so what you'll see in the survey is the presentation of both sets of results, which shows quite a contrast. Um, so in the interest of time, um, I realize it's a long report. I'm gonna highlight some of the key data points, um, for you and then we can move into q and a.

So first, new Zealanders are clearly continuing to recognize the strategic importance of Asia to New Zealand's future. Asia remains the second most important region to New Zealand after Australia, and it's held this place. And since we started this asking this question 10 years ago today, four outta five New Zealanders. So 80% of New Zealanders see the Asia region as important or very important to New Zealand's future.

And we can see this importance reflected in growing knowledge levels, um, with New Zealanders who say they know at least a fair amount about Asia almost doubling in the past decade. Within Asia, north Asia has always been seen as the most important region to New New Zealand's future by a vast majority of New Zealanders.

So 80% in this year's survey, Southeast Asia is also on the rise at 4% to 51% of New Zealanders seeing the region as important. And South Asia held steady at 46% this survey, but it had had a bump of six percentage points in the previous year. At the country level, China and Japan are seen as the most important countries to New Zealand's future by quite a significant margin, followed by India, Singapore, and South Korea.

And these rankings remain unchanged from last year. Turning to friend and threat perceptions. New Zealanders are showing increased weariness of great powers, um, but also an appreciation of growing partnerships with other countries in the region. I think you see that in the results of this survey this year.

We saw warm, we saw warming sentiment, uh, towards all countries in Asia, with the exception of North Korea, uh, which New Zealanders still see as the most threatening country in the region. Uh, Japan continues to be seen as New Zealand's closest friend, um, and has been since we started asking the question in 2017. This matches similar polling results in Australia, also where Japan ranks top in the region.

Japan has followed by Singapore, the Philippines, and South Korea. Again, a result unchanged from last year. But what has changed is the results for the South Asia Southeast Asia region. Um, all Southeast Asian countries received a boost of eight to 10 percentage points in friends sentiment this year as good South Korea.

So the demonstrating the growing warmth towards these countries, and I think a growing recognition of their strategic importance for all of these countries, growing people to people links through tourism, education, immigration, and of course New Zealanders love for Asian food. Um, a key to contributing to warming perceptions and deeper friendships.

And you can see this actually in the rapidly, uh, warming sentiment towards the Philippines, where friend perceptions have increased 19% over the past five years alone. New Zealanders are also showing continued wariness of great powers. As I mentioned, over the past few years, views of China have been mixed, and this year is no different.

Um, although China is seen as the most important country to New Zealand's future, it is also seen as a net threat in our March mini poll results with 41% seeing it as a threat and 21% as a friend. Uh, threatening countries in the survey is are defined, uh, as those viewed with caution and suspicion.

And perceptions are often informed by events in the world, by behaviors internationally. And we see perceptions often spike in response to events as they happen. And we can see this in the results, uh, for the United States, which also showed a significant drop in friends sentiment. By March of this year, pre perceptions of the US dropped from 61% to 32%, and the same proportion of New Zealanders see the United States as a threat.

Um, this again matches polling results that we've seen in Australia and also around the world. Unsurprisingly, trust in both major powers to act responsibly in the world is at an all time low. While Japan, uh, continues to be the country. New Zealanders trust the most in the region, again, matching results from Australia and also polling from Southeast Asia where they see Japan as the the most responsible actor.

Uh, while trade ties remain a priority for relationship building, new Zealanders are seeing broader economic opportunities in technology and innovation hubs, as well as in sec, um, defense and security cooperation. So the perceived importance of investing energy and resources in building partnerships with Asian countries is up 9% this year with 92%.

Seeing this as at least somewhat important in terms of where New Zealanders see potential. Singapore, South Korea and Japan are all seen as offering trade opportunities as well as being hubs for technology and innovation. And India is identified as an important trade opportunity as well as a rising power. And if we look at defense and security partnerships, Japan, South Korea and Singapore, again rise to the top.

And these rankings were unchanged from the previous survey, but notably this year, um, each country is up nine to 10 percentage points and importance, again, reflecting recognition of the importance of having partnerships across economic, political and security issues. Uh, continuing the defense and security theme, we asked a few additional questions this year reflecting the changing international environment.

Um, one was around defense spending. Um, when it comes to defense spending, 37% of New Zealanders believe the government spends the right amount on defense, 35% thinks, um, it should spend more. While only 11% of New Zealanders think, um, that New Zealand should spend less on defense. When, um, asked about New Zealanders views of orus, there hadn't been public polling on ORUS when we issued the survey in November and December.

And so we included it in this survey at the, um, the results were mixed, um, but tend more positive than negative. 35% of New Zealanders think the arrangement will make the Asia of region safer, while only 7% think it will make the region less safe. In terms of what New Zealanders are most worried about, um, in terms of threats to vital interests, fake news and disinformation, climate change and cyber attacks continue to rank as the top grief threats to vital interests.

This actually contrasts with Australia, where concern about a military conflict in the region ranks much higher at second in their list compared to 11th for us. And a couple of final points now, um, on Asia related literacy and media consumption. So the survey shows New Zealanders continue to recognize the importance of having Asia related skills and capability for the future of New Zealand's workforce.

So 92% of New Zealanders think that having confidence in dealing with people from Asian cultures is at least somewhat important. And 76% of New Zealanders feel at least fairly confident in dealing with people of Asian backgrounds. Um, in terms of language learning, Mandarin and Japanese are the languages that New Zealanders would most like to learn if there is an opportunity to do so.

Though the perceived importance of understanding Asian languages, cultures and societies has declined over the past couple of years, and this is a trend we're tracking since ultimately these are the skills that enable people to feel confident and engaging, um, in Asia. Finally, where are the perceptions coming from and how are they being shaped?

Uh, it's clear that online media will become the dominant, um, source of news on Asia for New Zealanders, and that social media accounts for an increasingly significant proportion of that, especially among younger age groups. So as a foundation, we're thinking hard about what this means for knowledge levels, um, on Asia, how we can supplement social media as a source of information, especially again for the younger people of New Zealand.

Um, the Asian Media Center obviously plays a crucial role there as New Zealand's de only dedicated, um, Asian news source, as does our outreach and engagement across universities in New Zealand. So that concludes my brief summary of results relevant to this committee. Um, just to flag a couple of activities for the research program coming up.

We spent many years, um, surveying New Zealanders views of Asia. Um, we have, um, pilot results of a survey looking at Southeast Asian views of New Zealand to flip the survey around. So we're looking forward to releasing those results in the next couple of months. Um, and given the pace of change in the world around us, we're also leaning more heavily into more frequent mini polls and to try and track New Zealander's views, uh, more frequently over the year rather than these simple annual snapshots that we produce.

So I'll stop there and we're happy to answer any questions that you might have. Fascinating. Thank you very much for that insight, Connor. Uh, look, thank you very much. You're very, very good. Um, do you, do you think that um, perhaps we, we are at risk of not being able to see the trees for the wood? Um, usually the other way around that is that we are targeting Asia and it's made up of, of a number of different quite cultures that, that are different and that by taking an an Asia approach, we are not better identifying the opportunities in Indonesia

or if it's say Vietnam or, and so is it time for us to be a little more refined in our assessments in our relationships? Is that a question for New Zealand more broadly or for the foundation? Well, given the foundation is doing a lot of, I guess, um, foundation work, pardon, pardon, you know, around people's understanding of market opportunities.

Yeah. Do you think it's, it may be time for, for you or for other groups to break up into kind of more country specific analysis? Yeah. Um, a couple of thoughts there and Julie, you you'll no doubt have something much more sensible to say. But, um, the, for when we first started, it was a concern in 1994, um, that there is this huge important region that we have no sense of really and what's happening there.

We needed to upskill ourselves. So it really was a, what is this region? And let's go about it. Over the past 30 years, we have no question gone from, uh, Asia is this homogenous idea to now much more sophisticated, not only country, but sector specific information. What I would say though, for us, and when we are being quite small, one of our advantages to the system is being, um, able to move toward, um, where we see those opportunities.

Our eight director, you know, the heads of those programs are all leading experts on say, creative sector across Asia, entrepreneurship across Asia. And their, their level of being attuned to where they think they can take clusters of New Zealand doesn't really make an impact and then key them into those either sectors and countries.

Um, the other thing is the research tells us that when New Zealanders think Asia, they think China. And so for quite some time perceptions of Asia was designed to say, Hey, look, New Zealand, China is a really important partner, a very, very important trade partner, but don't forget all of these other countries now, as we see coming through in the research, thanks to efforts across so many sectors, um, we are now able to sort of stretch that out.

And that's why we do this is just the macro longitudinal data at every country and sector. We have much more specific information available. Does So, so if I could say that's a yes to my question, um, that that is that what should we do to better focus, um, you know, I know you have limited resources, but I mean we have NZTE, we have nfa, and you know, we have you doing some good work.

Um, what, what's your advice on how we can better understand, focus and take up the opportunities in, in each of those Asian markets? I mean both from a cultural, not just a transactional perspective. Yeah, I think we're getting much, much better at operating as NZ Inc.

Um, so one of the things we try and do is really make sure that like next week we will be doing a full year long plan ahead. What's NZT doing? What are their priorities? How do, how do we make sure we are complimentary to the effort? What are all the trends they're seeing? What are we seeing? How do we sort of join that up so that we're not duplicating and that we are really adding?

Can I interrupt because please, sorry Mr. Chairman time interrupt yourself. Just carry no on, on on the basis of NGT. They have a customer focus. Yep. And, and you have an Asia focus. I guess the question is how do we, given that China is still a focus and everyone talks of an overdependence, why are we not perhaps more quickly moving to some of those other market opportunities?

So once again, meaning New Zealand, Well, what advice could you give to actually, I guess expand the focus beyond China In Asia? So I think a lot comes down to, um, you know, we know from our research that how the, the government is leading on these important relationships.

So it's really important having a leader level and, um, all of you speaking about these important relationships. China's very important, but don't forget, um, so that definitely comes through in the research. Second is we know that New Zealanders are getting their information from a range of particularly online sources.

So we are trying to really make sure that we are providing curated data for New Zealanders saying look at these growth rates. Don't forget these other markets. But also our directors are keyed into those New Zealand sectors. So sometimes, you know, when we can't, we just simply can't with our numbers reach all of New Zealand.

So that's where we really try and work with industry leads and other decision makers who then can tell us who the critical players are. Thirdly, there is clearly I think an underinvestment in terms of the growing the pipeline. So we are doing what we can to grow that pipeline of Asia capable New Zealanders, but we are in the track two space across New Zealand's University dealing with an ever dwindling number of Asia experts.

Um, and that means that for those track two dialogues, for they our spokespeople too. Um, and if we can now count on one hand who they are, and in the past we've had a lot of them that that's worrying because then um, foreign experts will fill that void and we will have offshore experts saying this is, you know, because media will have to go offshore to find that expertise. Thank you.

Um, Ken, just to jump to, I think it's page 72 in your report in relation to defense and security concerns, um, interested firstly, whether you asked the question of the perception of likelihood of conflict or not, because your question there is seems to be there is conflict, where is it going to be in Asia?

Mm-hmm. You ask separately a question on do you think there will be conflict in Asia? Because looking at that, saying that 42% of people think that there's gonna trying around the next 10 years seems quite high. Mm-hmm. Um, and would certainly have an impact on relationship. Um, so yeah. Can you delve into that a little more? Yeah. It was about the likelihood of conflict occurring.

Um, and so you'll see there's about a quarter of New Zealanders who think it's likely to occur and, and they were given their pre-populated list. So they basically were ranking and those specific contingencies rather than coming up with them on their own. And so basically assessing the likelihood that they think that conflict would occur in the next 10 years.

So the Korean peninsula, the South China Sea, um, dispute Taiwan, um, and then a number said that they don't know, um, and a small percent said they didn't think any of those would likely happen. Right, okay. So the 9% is people that think there will be no conflict for The next 10 years? Yes, exactly right. It's a very small Number. It is a very small number, although I will say it's, um, it's interesting on the Taiwan results, which I think is 23%, although some of these numbers get blurry in my head after a while.

Um, but that's actually 61% in Australia who are concerned about that. 61%. 61%. They're Right. So Yeah. Can you just give us some insight on how those numbers here have shifted in the last Weeks? So that's not a longitudinal question for us. So unfortunately that's just this year, but we can retest it next year and see if that's changed.

So that's one of the ones that we added in this year. A new question? Yeah, Just, just can you just tell me who you are surveying? Mm, I was like really interested that they had such strong opinions. Yeah, no, it's true. I was surprised on that, on a number of the defense insecurity questions. Yeah. In particular. Um, so it is a representative sample of New Zealand.

We work with a survey company Ipsos, who um, collect a panel of New Zealanders 2,300, um, that are representative by region age, gender, ethnicity, and the, the views are weighted and that sample is weighted according to census data to make sure that it's representative of New Zealand. Are they selected for being well-informed?

No, no, they are absolutely. Yeah. Everybody, they are contracted to be out on the street New Zealand. Great. So we can break it down by education level for example. And we do that in some of the data that we represent to show what the, what the differences might be based on where you live, um, ethnicity or education levels to show how views might change.

Time for a couple of quick question. I know, I mean, There might be prisoners of the algorithm of all of us as well. I I was wondering about, um, what your report told you about Myanmar? Yeah. What you had on Myanmar. What about what, uh, what, what, what, what did you hear back about that? Not a lot to be honest.

There were a few countries that, um, new Zealanders don't know a huge amount about and Myanmar, Indonesia, that Indonesia falls in that bracket actually as well. Um, that there isn't, um, when you look at sort of relative importance to New Zealand and the limits, and last year when we asked about interest levels in learning more, um, percentages are quite low.

And so part of what we use this for is to guide the research and outreach program for the foundation to try and shine a light on those countries that New Zealanders don't show either much knowledge on or interest in learning about. But, and so, um, to supplement that this month we've hosted New Zealand's, um, leading New Zealand expert on Myanmar to and broaden all the leading representatives so that he's giving a candid frank account over a, a round table session to say what's the absolute latest in Myanmar, what's happening?

Um, what, what's happening across the border? What's happening with third country relations? We are writing about Myanmar on the Asian Media Center website. We did a podcast, hosting a podcast on Myanmar. So where we see the, it's not kind of on the radar, we then go into action mode ourselves using our programs. Uh, and I just, one, one other question you, you said earlier around that having leader to leader in conversations and stuff like that, what, what in your mind, what is a quality engagement?

Um, mm so for example, we have to speaker delegations going over to Yeah, it's a really good question On the, I can speak to the track two side. Um, when, when we go out and with our delegations, we're spending full days, um, with our counterparts in a very, uh, Chatham House free and frank environment where we can have very in depth discussions and a trusted environment where they know they can share their views and they're not going to be quoted or misused in any way.

And so I think it's the depth of those discussions that really is the value add that the foundation can bring back to the New Zealand system. And I think for more broadly for New Zealand, I think there's been impression that um, for many years that New Zealand foreign policy was trade policy. We heard feedback that we um, and traders existential for New Zealand.

So I don't think New Zealanders, you know, we can be fairly unashamed about our need to be connected to markets. Um, but I think we're getting much, much better at the relationship piece. Um, so really making sure that we are taking time to understand cultures and connect with a wider, um, cohort of connections across the region.

And that's where we can help because we have um, networks, but we've got senior contacts who are living in the region who can say, Hey look, if you're gonna bring together a wider, um, representation of people, I suggest you connect with these people. So, and then they stay in as champions in the market for us, we don't rotate.

So we are not like, um, mission, uh, embassies where every three years people are changing, you know, I'm Admir Julia and hers. So that too is sort of a strength. Um, our, a lot of our networks, we have people with us for 20 years. So it's a, uh, for um, that in itself I think is a bit of a, for New Zealand and Asia relations. Mm-hmm.

Yeah, absolutely. Alright, thank you very much. Unfortunately, we have come to end of our time, um, but really appreciate your insights there and I think it's a great idea that you're looking at some more mini surveys too. Mm-hmm. Obviously we've seen a lot of things happening. Yeah. Um, you know, from the defense side, Thailand, Cambodia and India, Pakistan, yeah. A lot In the last few months as well, so would potentially impact on that.
But um, yeah, I think we would be very keen to stay in touch as well. Great, great. As a committee. So thank you again for coming in. Thank you. Thank you. Cheers.
